about.intro: Senior Frontend Engineer with 4+ years building scalable enterprise web and mobile applications. Specializing in Angular, TypeScript, and clean architecture.
bio: Previously at ITGalax Solutions Pvt Ltd, I engineered government-grade portals and hybrid mobile apps serving 10,000+ active users — improving application performance by 50% and reducing API consumption by 40%. My expertise spans government portals (Fiji Immigration Department), enterprise pension management systems (PRIMS), and cross-platform mobile apps (Vanuatu National Provident Fund).

I specialize in Angular (v13–v21), TypeScript, RxJS, Angular Signals, Ionic Angular, and Tailwind CSS. I thrive in Agile/Scrum teams, deliver reusable component-driven architecture, and maintain clean, scalable codebases for enterprise environments. Open to remote, hybrid, and full-time opportunities across India.

featured.name: Galaxy Sofas
featured.outcome: E-commerce site that increased customer enquiries 3× through performance optimization
featured.description: A responsive, mobile-first e-commerce website for Galaxy Sofas featuring product browsing, smart filtering, and integrated enquiry system. Laser-focused on Core Web Vitals and conversion optimization.

caseStudy.context: Galaxy Sofas needed a modern web presence to compete with larger furniture retailers online. The client's main goal: convert site visitors into qualified enquiries.
caseStudy.problem: Existing Shopify store had critical performance issues — LCP of 4.2s, CLS of 0.3, and zero SEO ranking for local searches. Bounce rate on mobile was 68%.
caseStudy.solution: Rebuilt with Next.js 14 using Server Components for instant interactivity, image optimization with next/image, and a bespoke Tailwind design system. Integrated analytics to track which product categories drove enquiries.

challenges.problem: Dynamically generated product images (100+ sofas) were killing LCP
challenges.options: Use Shopify image CDN, pre-generate all variants, or lazy-load on scroll
challenges.chosen: Next.js Image with on-demand ISR + srcset — best of performance and freshness
challenges.why: Gave us instant LCP, saved 60% bandwidth vs unoptimized images, and ISR kept catalogue fresh without rebuilds

challenges.problem: Mobile conversion funnel dropped 40% between product view and enquiry submission
challenges.options: Redesign form, add trust badges, or implement progressive disclosure
challenges.chosen: Progressive disclosure with multi-step mobile form + social proof carousel
challenges.why: Reduced cognitive load on mobile — form went from 8 fields to 2–3 per step, conversion rate improved 35%

challenges.problem: SEO traffic was zero; category pages weren't ranking
challenges.options: Manual sitemap, dynamic schema, or structured category pages with content
challenges.chosen: Dynamic schema.org markup + dedicated category landing pages with contextual content
challenges.why: Ranked for 'sofas in [city]' within 6 weeks; organic traffic became 25% of all enquiries

gallery.alt: Product listing with Web Vitals metrics

featured.alt: Zellavora Technologies — Corporate website with design system
featured.name: Zellavora Technologies
featured.outcome: Brand website showcasing company identity and driving 40+ qualified leads monthly
featured.description: The official corporate website for Zellavora Technologies, built with a full design system, SSR for SEO, and a modern component-driven architecture. Demonstrates premium brand positioning and technical depth.

caseStudy.role: Full-stack: Design System Architecture, SSR Implementation, Lead Frontend
caseStudy.context: Zellavora needed a premium web presence that matched the quality of its products. The brand message: 'Enterprise-grade, thoughtfully designed, built for scale.' Website had to do three jobs: communicate brand, drive lead capture, and showcase technical credibility.
caseStudy.problem: No cohesive online brand; SSR was critical for SEO but not standard in Angular at that time.
caseStudy.solution: Built a three-tier design token system, integrated Angular Universal for full SSR, and created a reusable component library that's used across all Zellavora products.

challenges.problem: Design system needed to work in both SSR and CSR without duplication
challenges.options: Separate style bundles, CSS-in-JS, or pure CSS custom properties
challenges.chosen: CSS custom properties + Angular DI for theme switching, with build-time critical CSS extraction
challenges.why: Zero runtime overhead, works without JS, and the theme system became a template for Zellavora UI

challenges.problem: Angular SSR had unstable hydration with dynamic directives
challenges.options: Pre-render everything, use @defer for dynamic content, or build a custom hydration guard
challenges.chosen: Custom pre-render strategy + @defer for below-fold components
challenges.why: Gave us 100% Lighthouse scores, instant hydration, and a reusable pattern for other apps

challenges.problem: Brand story needed to feel premium without being slow
challenges.options: Video hero, heavy animations, or strategic use of motion
challenges.chosen: Ambient CSS animations on glass morphism cards, subtle scroll-triggered reveals
challenges.why: Converted better than video (61% scroll-through vs 40% video watch), and kept LCP under 1.5s

gallery.alt: Zellavora hero section with glass morphism

featured.name: Zellavora UI
featured.outcome: Published npm package with 15+ production-ready components and 100% accessibility compliance
featured.description: An open-source Angular component library built with accessibility-first principles, published on npm with full TypeScript support, interactive documentation, and live component previews.

caseStudy.role: Library Architect, A11y Lead, npm Publishing
caseStudy.context: Zellavora needed a reusable component library that could be shared across team projects and publicly consumed by the Angular community. The library had to set a new standard: accessibility-first, well-documented, and genuinely useful.
caseStudy.problem: Building a quality open-source library requires solving API design, a11y, theming, documentation, and npm publishing — each a specialty. Most Angular libraries are either over-engineered or missing a11y.
caseStudy.solution: Three-tier token architecture with WCAG 2.1 AA compliance baked in. Every component has keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, and tested focus management. Published to npm with full TypeScript support and live component storybook.

challenges.problem: 15 components with different a11y concerns (form inputs, dialogs, dropdowns, etc.)
challenges.options: Implement a11y on each component separately, or build a shared accessibility toolkit
challenges.chosen: FocusTrap service + FormField wrapper component + centralized ARIA labeling mixin
challenges.why: Reduced a11y code duplication by 70%, made it impossible to forget aria-labels, and made testing consistent

challenges.problem: API design — should theming be CSS-only, component inputs, or both?
challenges.options: CSS custom properties only, Angular Signals for theme switching, or hybrid
challenges.chosen: CSS custom properties as the source of truth, Angular service for programmatic switching
challenges.why: Works for both CSS-only users and those needing runtime theme switching, zero lock-in

challenges.problem: npm package needed to work in Angular 14+ with no breaking changes for 2 years
challenges.options: Lock to Angular 21, use semver carefully, or build compatibility layer
challenges.chosen: Strict semantic versioning with compatibility matrix, documented deprecation cycle
challenges.why: Users can upgrade on their timeline; library stayed at 1.x for 18 months with zero breakage

featured.alt: Zellavora AI Resume Builder — AI-powered resume generation
featured.name: Zellavora AI Resume Builder
featured.outcome: AI-powered resume builder helping job seekers create ATS-optimized resumes in 5 minutes
featured.description: A React SPA that pairs a guided resume builder with Claude API integration for AI-assisted content generation. Live preview, ATS-optimized templates, and one-click PDF export.

caseStudy.role: Full-stack: Product Design, API Integration, Frontend Lead
caseStudy.context: Recruiters in 2026 actively look for evidence of AI integration in senior candidates' portfolios. Resume builders exist, but none pair AI-assisted content with ATS optimization. We built this to solve a real job-seeker pain point while showcasing applied AI.
caseStudy.problem: Writing a strong resume is hard. Most AI tools generate generic fluff. Job seekers need: smart content suggestions based on their role history, ATS-friendly formatting, and fast exports.
caseStudy.solution: React SPA with a guided form that calls Claude API to generate job-specific bullet points, outcome-focused summaries, and skill descriptions. Live preview syncs with ATS-friendly templates. One-click PDF export.

challenges.problem: Claude API calls are expensive at scale; cost per user export ~$0.50
challenges.options: Cache responses, limit free tier, or switch to cheaper models
challenges.chosen: Hybrid: free tier uses on-device summarization (transformers.js), Claude for premium
challenges.why: Keeps free users unblocked, premium users get better quality, and we control costs

challenges.problem: AI-generated content is sometimes verbose or off-brand
challenges.options: Fine-tune a model, add post-generation filtering, or let users edit
challenges.chosen: Streaming responses with user edit controls + refinement prompts
challenges.why: Keeps AI as a starting point, not final output; users stay in control

challenges.problem: ATS systems hate fancy formatting; live preview vs ATS output can look different
challenges.options: Strip all formatting, use minimal CSS, or build ATS parser
challenges.chosen: Dual preview: visual (what they submit) + ATS view (what parsers see)
challenges.why: Users can see exactly what an ATS will read; confidence is highest converter

gallery.alt: Live preview with ATS compatibility view

featured.alt: Zellavora Portfolio — WCAG 2.1 AA Compliance Case Study
featured.name: Zellavora Portfolio — Accessibility Audit & Implementation
featured.outcome: Audited and fixed 12 accessibility issues, achieving WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across light and dark modes with comprehensive automated testing
featured.description: This portfolio website, rebuilt with a systematic accessibility-first approach. Includes WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, dual-theme testing, keyboard navigation, and a full Playwright test suite demonstrating production-grade engineering practices.

caseStudy.role: Full-stack: Accessibility Audit, Test Suite Design, Performance Lead
caseStudy.context: Enterprise companies increasingly require WCAG 2.1 AA compliance from vendors and contractors. A portfolio demonstrating genuine accessibility knowledge — not just claims — signals senior-level engineering maturity. Most portfolios ignore light mode contrast, animation preferences, and keyboard navigation.
caseStudy.problem: Audit revealed 12 accessibility issues: animations ignored prefers-reduced-motion, light mode gradient text failed 4.5:1 contrast, no landmark navigation, mobile nav lacked focus management. Additionally: no robots.txt, no XML sitemap, inconsistent testing across themes.
caseStudy.solution: Implemented Phase 1-3 accessibility overhaul: (1) Motion preferences respected globally via shouldReduceEffects() helper, (2) Light mode token system with 4.5:1+ contrast, (3) Semantic landmarks with aria-labelledby on all sections, (4) Mobile nav focus trap with Esc/Tab keyboard controls, (5) Theme-aware axe-core scanner utility, (6) Playwright dual-theme test suite, (7) robots.txt and XML sitemap for SEO.

challenges.problem: Animations scattered across 4 directives (aurora, grid, magnetic, scroll) each checking prefers-reduced-motion independently
challenges.options: Create helper function vs. add to each directive vs. use Angular interceptor
challenges.chosen: Centralized shouldReduceEffects() helper + consistent refactor across all animations
challenges.why: Single source of truth, eliminates inconsistency, easy to test, easy to maintain

challenges.problem: Light mode gradient text had 2.1:1 contrast (fails WCAG AA, needs 4.5:1). Dark mode was fine at 6.3:1.
challenges.options: Switch to solid color, use different gradient, or implement @media prefers-color-scheme
challenges.chosen: Theme-aware gradient via CSS @media query + updated all light mode color tokens
challenges.why: Preserves design in dark mode, unlocks full feature set in light mode, demonstrates theme-aware thinking

challenges.problem: Mobile nav: Tab focus would escape menu, no way to close with keyboard
challenges.options: Restrict focus via shadow DOM, implement manual focus trap, or use ARIA modal
challenges.chosen: Custom focus trap in header.ts + Esc key listener with proper focus restoration
challenges.why: Works with Angular's change detection, reusable pattern, tested with Playwright

challenges.problem: Accessibility testing only on dark mode; light mode contrast issues slip through
challenges.options: Manual testing both themes, or automated testing with theme switching
challenges.chosen: Playwright tests with emulateMedia({ colorScheme: 'light' / 'dark' }) + axe-core scanner
challenges.why: Catches both themes automatically, prevents regressions, scales to all pages

grid.alt: Fiji Immigration Department — Enterprise Immigration Management System
grid.name: Fiji Immigration Department — Enterprise Immigration Management System
grid.description: A large-scale government immigration platform serving the Fiji Immigration Department. Engineered multiple role-based portals including External User Portal, Internal Staff Portal, Police Verification Portal, and a Payment Gateway.
grid.highlights: Engineered 4 role-based portals with JWT Auth and RBAC, Built complex multi-step forms, enterprise validation modules, and workflow systems, Developed and published shared Angular component libraries used across all portals, Integrated REST APIs and backend Web API services across the entire platform, Delivered responsive, mobile-friendly interfaces for large-scale government workflows.

caseStudy.problem: The Fiji Immigration Department relied on outdated paper-based workflows and fragmented legacy systems, causing processing delays of 3–5 business days per application. Staff had no unified digital view of applicant status, resulting in manual follow-ups and high error rates.
caseStudy.approach: Built a multi-portal Angular 21 SPA with role-based access control — separate portals for external applicants, internal immigration officers, and police verification units. Used Angular Signals for real-time UI state and integrated a secure payment gateway for permit fees.
challenges: Complex multi-role RBAC with 6 distinct user types across 3 separate portal UIs
Integrating legacy government APIs with inconsistent data formats
Ensuring WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility compliance for a government system

grid.alt: PRIMS — Pension & Retirement Information Management System
grid.name: PRIMS — Pension & Retirement Information Management System
grid.description: An enterprise pension management platform for HR teams and administrators, handling retirement information and administrative workflows. Built using Zoneless Angular for next-generation performance.
grid.highlights: Built responsive dashboards and reusable UI modules for enterprise administration, Enhanced rendering using Angular Signals and optimized change detection strategies, Reduced API consumption via RxJS caching and stream optimization, Architected scalable, maintainable frontend structure for high-traffic enterprise usage, Improved frontend maintainability through a fully reusable component system.

caseStudy.problem: Legacy pension system had critical performance bottlenecks — dashboard rendered 1,200+ records without virtualisation, causing 8-second load times on mid-range hardware used by administrators.
caseStudy.approach: Rebuilt the dashboard with Angular 21 Zoneless change detection, CDK virtual scroll for large datasets, and an API caching layer using RxJS shareReplay. Introduced reusable enterprise component library for consistent UI across 12 dashboard modules.
challenges: Migrating from Angular 14 zone-based to Angular 21 zoneless without breaking existing RBAC logic
Handling 50,000+ pension records with real-time filtering and sorting
Coordinating with legacy backend teams on API contract standardisation

grid.alt: VNPF — Vanuatu National Provident Fund App
grid.name: VNPF — Vanuatu National Provident Fund App
grid.description: A cross-platform hybrid mobile application for the Vanuatu National Provident Fund, delivering insurance and provident fund services to citizens on Android and iOS in the Pacific region.
grid.highlights: Built full Android & iOS app using Ionic Angular and Capacitor, Implemented biometric authentication — Face ID and Touch ID, Optimized API communication for low-bandwidth Pacific region environments, Developed responsive mobile UI components and an admin portal module, Delivered seamless cross-platform experience with high accessibility standards.

caseStudy.problem: Fund members in Vanuatu had no mobile access to their provident fund accounts. Manual branch visits were the only way to check balances, causing long queues and poor member experience, especially on outer islands with limited connectivity.
caseStudy.approach: Built a cross-platform Ionic Angular app (Android + iOS) with offline-capable data caching for low-bandwidth environments. Integrated Capacitor biometric plugin for Face ID / Touch ID authentication and optimised API payloads for 2G/3G conditions.
challenges: Implementing secure biometric authentication that degrades gracefully on older Android devices
Designing offline mode with reliable cache invalidation for balance data
App Store and Google Play submission and compliance review cycles

blog.title: Angular deep-dives & engineering insights
blog.subtitle: Sharing what I learn building enterprise Angular applications — performance, architecture, and modern patterns.

articles.title: Angular Signals vs RxJS: When to Use Each in Enterprise Apps
articles.excerpt: After migrating three enterprise Angular apps from zone-based RxJS patterns to Signals-first architecture, here's what I've learned about where each shines — and where mixing them is the right call.

content.text: Signals landed as a stable API and immediately split teams into two camps: rip out RxJS entirely, or ignore Signals and keep shipping. After migrating three enterprise apps, I've found the truth is more nuanced — they solve overlapping but distinct problems.

A Signal is a synchronous container for a value that notifies consumers when it changes. An Observable is a stream of values over time, with a rich operator vocabulary for combining, throttling, and cancelling asynchronous work. Signals model state; Observables model events.

items: Component state that drives the template — no async pipe, no manual subscription, no OnPush friction.
Derived state via computed(), which memoizes automatically and only recalculates when a dependency actually changes.
Reading the current value synchronously — no more BehaviorSubject just to call getValue().
Anything time-based: debounceTime, throttleTime, retry with backoff.
Coordinating multiple async sources — switchMap on a typeahead is still unbeaten.
Event streams where you care about the sequence, not just the latest value.

content.text: The bridge is toSignal() and toObservable(). Keep async orchestration in RxJS, then convert to a Signal at the edge where the template consumes it:

items: Start with local component state — the lowest-risk, highest-reward conversion.
Leave HTTP and cross-cutting streams in RxJS until the Signals resource APIs mature.
Don't convert for the sake of it: a working switchMap does not need to become a Signal.

content.text: Bottom line: Signals are the default for state, RxJS is the specialist for asynchronous event streams. In enterprise apps you'll use both — the skill is knowing which layer each belongs in.

articles.title: The Angular Performance Audit Checklist I Use on Every Project
articles.excerpt: A practical, battle-tested checklist for identifying and fixing the most common Angular performance bottlenecks — from change detection to bundle size to Core Web Vitals.

content.text: Most Angular performance problems are not exotic. They cluster around a handful of recurring mistakes. This is the checklist I run on every project, top to bottom.

items: Adopt OnPush on every component — it's the single highest-leverage change.
Move toward a zoneless setup where the app is Signals-driven.
Never call functions that do real work in templates; they run on every check.
Lazy-load every route with loadComponent / loadChildren.
Audit with source-map-explorer — find the one dependency bloating your main chunk.
Prefer standalone components and tree-shakeable providers.
Always use track in @for — untracked lists re-create the entire DOM subtree.
Virtualize long lists with the CDK virtual scroll.
Use NgOptimizedImage for automatic lazy-loading, srcset, and priority hints.
Set priority on the LCP image so it isn't lazy-loaded by accident.
Cache idempotent GETs and deduplicate in-flight requests with shareReplay.

content.text: Run Lighthouse in CI, not just locally, and budget the three Core Web Vitals that actually matter:

content.code:
# LCP  < 2.5s   (largest contentful paint)
# INP  < 200ms  (interaction to next paint)
# CLS  < 0.1    (cumulative layout shift)
npx unlighthouse --site https://your-site.com

content.text: Fix in this order — change detection and bundle size usually reclaim more than every micro-optimization combined.

articles.title: Implementing Biometric Auth in Ionic Angular with Capacitor
articles.excerpt: A step-by-step guide to integrating Face ID and Touch ID in Ionic Angular apps using the Capacitor biometric plugin — including graceful fallback for older devices.

content.text: Biometric authentication turns a clunky password prompt into a one-tap unlock. In Ionic Angular with Capacitor it's a small amount of code — the hard part is doing the fallback and error handling correctly.
Never assume the device has Face ID or a fingerprint sensor. Probe capability before you offer the option:

items: Always keep a password/PIN path — biometrics can fail or be unenrolled.
Set allowDeviceCredential so the OS offers the device passcode automatically.
After repeated failures the OS locks biometrics; detect the error and route to password.
Biometrics gate access to a secret you already store securely — they are not the secret itself.
Store tokens in the Keychain / Keystore, never in localStorage.
Treat a biometric success as one factor, not proof of identity for sensitive server actions.

content.text: Done right, this is the rare feature that improves both security and UX at once — provided the fallback path is as polished as the happy path.

As a freelancer, I can transition to a full-time role with flexible notice, typically within 2-4 weeks depending on current project commitments.
Open to discussion based on role, location, and compensation package. Happy to discuss during the interview process.

faqs.q: Can you work across time zones?
faqs.a: Yes, I'm in IST (UTC+5:30) but comfortable working with teams across any time zone with reasonable overlap.

faqs.q: What types of roles interest you?
faqs.a: Senior Frontend Engineer, Angular Developer, Full Stack Engineer, or Technical Lead roles in enterprise, government, or product-based companies.

notAFit: Roles requiring relocation outside preferred locations without flexibility
Projects with no technical growth opportunity
